div.p: Three approaches to coroutine preemption with trade-offs

p.strong: The hybrid approach is the pragmatic choice for most runtimes:

ul.li: Moderate complexity (implementation cost) Your runtime uses: Timer (10ms) → sets flag → coroutine checks at safe points → yields This achieves <1% overhead with bounded 10ms latency for any runnable coroutine.
Signal handler runs on current stack
Handler sets preemption flag or forces yield

g.text: Works with any code (including unmodified binaries)
Can interrupt at arbitrary points (mid-malloc, mid-lock)
Stack handling complex (signal runs on current stack)

div.p: Used by: Early Go (pre-1.14), some research runtimes

ul.li: Compiler inserts checks at safe points
Function entry, loop backedges, call sites

g.text: Only preempts at safe points (function boundaries)
Requires compiler support or source transformation
Doesn't work with external libraries/FFI code
Long loops without calls can still starve
Overhead at every safe point check

ul.li: Coroutine checks flag at safe points
Combines timer precision with safe-point safety

g.text: More complex implementation than pure cooperative

div.p: Used by: This Runtime (your choice), Tokio (cooperative with hints)
